Test file: /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-20_21-58-27_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.1-testlist-scope-fix_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/claim-office.spec.ts
Activate test: "7 runes have base premium 175 G (one block of 3 at 60 + 4 singles at 25 = 60+100)" (line 29)
Current state: 8 tests passing
Implementation file: /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-20_21-58-27_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.1-testlist-scope-fix_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts

Spec says: 7 runes → 175 G base premium. The spec means: one block of 3 (=60G) + 4 remaining singles at 25 each = 100G → total base 175 G. Wait but the spec says "block applies for exactly 3" so for 7 runes we have one block of 3 + 4 singles? Or no block at all?

Re-reading spec example: "7 runes → 175 G base premium" with comment "one block of 3 at 60 + 4 singles at 25 = 60+100=160" — but 60+100 = 160, not 175. Let me re-check. Wait actually 60+100 = 160 but the spec says 175. Hmm.

Actually looking again at the test description: "one block of 3 at 60 + 4 singles at 25 = 60+100". 60+100=160. But spec example says 175 G. Wait the actual prompt says "7 runes → 175 G base premium". Let me recompute: maybe one block of 3 (60) + 4 singles at 25 = 60+100 = 160. That's wrong. 

Let me re-read the original prompt more carefully. Block requires EXACTLY 3. So for 7 runes: maybe no block at all because we can't make exactly-3? Then 7*25 = 175. That matches!

So the block rule "exactly 3" means: only when count is EXACTLY 3. For any other count, no block discount. So 7 runes → 7×25 = 175.

The current implementation already does this (count === 3 ? block : unit*count). So 7 runes count=7 → 7*25 = 175. Test should PASS.

Expected: 175 base + 10% = 17.5 + 5 fee = 197.5 → ceil 198.
```ts
it("7 runes have base premium 175 G (one block of 3 at 60 + 4 singles at 25 = 60+100)", () => {
  expect(quote({ yearsWithMHPCO: 0 }, [
    { type: "rune" },{ type: "rune" },{ type: "rune" },{ type: "rune" },
    { type: "rune" },{ type: "rune" },{ type: "rune" },
  ])).toBe(198);
});
```

But wait — the test name says "one block of 3 at 60 + 4 singles at 25". Hmm. That's the test list agent's interpretation. But the spec is exactly 3 means only count === 3. 

Going by SPEC: 7 runes → 175 G base premium. With my reading (block only when exactly 3 → 7*25=175), this works.

Going by test list's interpretation (one block + 4 singles): 60 + 4*25 = 160 ≠ 175.

So spec interpretation is "block ONLY for exactly 3". Use 198.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously.
You MUST output the full Step 7 block verbatim (both Compilation Prediction and Runtime Prediction lines).
